step_by_step_thinking: I agree with the conclusion drawn in Round 1. One additional point supporting why mandibular changes are not prominently featured in Down's syndrome includes the fact that while there might be some variability in bone structure, the typical presentation focuses more on maxillofacial proportions like maxillary hypoplasia and overall mid-face flatness. Therefore, mandibular retrognathia (option B), mandibular prognathism (option C), and maxillary hypoplasia (option D) would stand out as distinguishing features if observed clinically, further reinforcing option B as the best fit based on established criteria for diagnosing Down's syndrome. Hence, my agreement remains firm, and the chosen answer stands validly as B. Final
